title: The Interesting Narrative of the Life of Olaudah Equiano, Or Gustavus Vassa, The African: Written By Himself
author: Equiano, Olaudah
summary: Olaudah Equiano's autobiography recounts his capture in Africa, description of his native customs, and the brutality of the slave trade that tore him from family. He describes horrors aboard slave ships, transportation to Virginia, arrival in England, and bewilderment at snow. Subsequent chapters narrate naval engagements, varied cruelties, his business ventures starting with threepence, schemes for freedom, religious conversion to Christianity, shipwreck on the Bahama Bank, arrival at Martinico, and return to England. Equiano seeks to awaken compassion, support abolition, and petitions the Crown; the work mixes personal memoir, ethnography, and political appeal. It combines vivid detail and moral urgency.

characters.name: Olaudah Equiano (Gustavus Vassa)
characters.age: born 1745 (age varies across narrative); adult during events
characters.lifespan: Narrates being kidnapped, enslaved, converted to Christianity, undertakes business ventures, seeks freedom, survives shipwrecks, returns to England and petitions the Crown.

characters.name: Equiano's sister
characters.age: child / same generation as Equiano at time of kidnapping (unnamed)
characters.personality: presented as a tender family relation; briefly referenced
characters.lifespan: Kidnapped with Equiano; fate in narrative is noted as part of his early trauma.

characters.name: Admiral Boscawen
characters.personality: commanding, celebrated (appears in an engagement recounted)
characters.lifespan: Referenced in a chapter giving a particular account of his naval engagement with Monsieur Le Clue.

characters.name: Monsieur Le Clue
characters.personality: opposing naval officer in engagement with Boscawen
characters.lifespan: Recounted as participant in the naval engagement described in the narrative.

characters.name: Captain John Clarkson
characters.personality: noted as a subscriber and maritime personage
characters.lifespan: Listed among subscribers; associated with maritime reform circles in context.

characters.name: The Reverend Thomas Clarkson
characters.lifespan: Listed as subscriber; historically active in abolition advocacy referenced by the work.

characters.name: Ottobah Cugoano (John Stewart)
characters.personality: respected, fellow African voice in Britain (listed)
characters.lifespan: Included among subscribers; recognized as a notable African and abolitionist contemporary.

characters.name: Priests / magicians (native priests)
characters.personality: ritual specialists; authoritative within native spiritual practices
characters.lifespan: Described in chapter on native superstition and funeral ceremonies, including roles in poison detection and funerals.

characters.name: The Queen (monarch)
characters.personality: figure of authority before whom petitions may be presented
characters.lifespan: Referred to as the recipient of a petition by the author in concluding chapters.

characters.name: William, son of Ignatius Sancho
characters.personality: notable as the son of Ignatius Sancho; a symbolically important name in subscriber list
characters.lifespan: Named in the subscribers; included in front matter but not as a narrative character.

locations.name: Bahama Bank (Great Bahama Bank, general area)
